title: assertResourceScope fails OPEN when the resource's association is unresolved (null/undefined) — cross-tenant read/write of null-association resources across 9 resource types
summary: The tenant-isolation helper assertResourceScope short-circuits to ALLOW whenever the resolver getAssociationIdForScopedResource returns a falsy association id. This exactly confirms the mapper hypothesis: an unresolved/undefined association silently bypasses the scope check. The resolver returns undefined in two ways — (a) the resource row's associationId column is NULL, and (b) an unknown/typo'd resourceType hits the switch `default: return undefined`. 9 of the 39 assertResourceScope-protected resource types have a NULLABLE associationId in the schema (governance_compliance_templates, clause_records, notice_templates, notice_sends, communication_history, ai_ingestion_jobs, ai_extracted_records, ai_ingestion_import_runs, permission_envelopes), so real rows with association_id = NULL (e.g. shared 'state-library'/'ct-baseline' governance templates, orphaned records) are reachable and mutable from ANY tenant's admin. Concrete write route: PATCH /api/governance/templates/:id is gated only by assertResourceScope + a partial guard that fires only if the client includes a `scope` field in the body.
impact: A non-platform-admin operator of tenant A can read and mutate any resource of the 9 protected types whose association_id is NULL — including shared/global governance compliance templates (affecting all associations), clause records, notice templates/sends, communication history and AI-extracted records that may contain sensitive extracted content. The DELETE counterpart (server/routes.ts:9242) is affected identically. Note: rows with a NON-null associationId are still correctly protected (resolver returns the real owner id and assertAssociationScope throws) — so this is a fail-open confined to null-association / unknown-type rows, not a blanket IDOR. Contrast with the sibling assertAssociationScope which was explicitly hardened to fail CLOSED on empty scope (routes.ts:1183-1187); assertResourceScope was not given the same treatment.
validation: Read assertResourceScope (routes.ts:1213-1218) and the full resolver switch (storage.ts:14528-14771) including the default case. Enumerated all 39 resourceType literals passed to assertResourceScope and confirmed each maps to a switch case today (no live unknown-type bypass) but the default remains a latent bypass. Cross-referenced schema nullability (shared/schema.ts) to identify 9 protected types with nullable associationId. Traced one concrete write route (PATCH governance/templates/:id) whose only isolation gate is assertResourceScope plus two conditionally-triggered guards.
remediation_direction: Make assertResourceScope fail CLOSED: when the resolver returns null/undefined, DENY (throw 'resource not found / outside scope') rather than return — mirroring the assertAssociationScope hardening. Handle legitimately-global rows (null association by design, e.g. platform-provided state-library templates) with an explicit allow-list branch keyed on resourceType + a platform-admin/read-only rule, not by a blanket falsy-allow. Add an explicit `default:` in getAssociationIdForScopedResource that throws on unknown resourceType so typos fail loudly instead of silently allowing.
regression_risk: Medium — flipping to fail-closed will start rejecting requests that today silently pass on null-association rows or on any not-yet-created id; legitimate flows that read/write genuinely-global templates (state-library/ct-baseline) must be preserved via the explicit allow-list branch, and any handler that calls assertResourceScope before creating a not-yet-existent resource must be reviewed.
tests_required: non-platform admin PATCH/DELETE of a governance template with association_id=NULL is denied
non-platform admin cannot read/mutate a clause-record/notice-template/communication-history row with null association
unknown resourceType passed to assertResourceScope throws rather than allows
platform-admin still allowed on global templates
legitimate same-tenant resource still passes
limitations: Static read-only analysis; did not run the app. Live exploit magnitude depends on the actual presence of association_id=NULL rows in each of the 9 tables (data-state dependent) — the count/sensitivity of such rows needs runtime verification against a real DB. The not-found-id path is benign for reads (the handler's own by-id query also returns nothing); only the null-association-row and unknown-type paths are actively dangerous.

title: records-requests module: complete absence of tenant isolation — cross-tenant IDOR read+write across all 7 routes (CT §47-260 owner records)
summary: Every route in server/routes/records-requests.ts is gated by requireAdmin + requireAdminRole (role check only) but performs NO association-scope check whatsoever — no assertAssociationScope, no assertResourceScope, no getAssociationIdQuery. (1) GET /api/records-requests reads a raw, unvalidated req.query.associationId; when omitted, storage.getRecordsRequests(undefined) returns ALL associations' records requests (storage.ts:7276-7277). (2) GET /:id, PATCH /:id, POST /:id/copy-fee, POST /:id/items, GET /:id/disclosure all fetch/update by the raw path id via storage.getRecordsRequest(id)/updateRecordsRequest(id) which query WHERE id = ? with no association filter — classic IDOR. (3) POST spreads ...req.body into the insert, so a client-supplied associationId lets an admin create records requests inside another tenant. The module header even claims 'Multi-tenant: every query scoped by associationId' (line 11) — the code does not honor that claim.
impact: Any authenticated admin (READ_ROLES incl. viewer for reads; WRITE_ROLES for writes) of ANY tenant can: enumerate/read every association's Connecticut §47-260 records requests and their disclosure partitions (owner PII, withheld/mandatory record classifications), read another tenant's request by id, modify status/fees/exam-dates on another tenant's request, attach items, and create records requests inside an arbitrary tenant. This is a deterministic cross-tenant confidentiality + integrity breach on a compliance/legal-records surface.
validation: Read the full module (records-requests.ts:69-221) confirming no scope helper is called on any handler; read the four storage methods (storage.ts:7275-7334) confirming by-id queries filter on id only and getRecordsRequests(undefined) returns all rows. Confirmed via the module's own scope-ref count (7 routes, 1 scope-ref — the lone ref being the unused adminScopedAssociationIds type field).
remediation_direction: Introduce a mandatory association scope on every route: for list, resolve the tenant via the central getAssociationIdQuery(req) (which validates the requested id against req.adminScopedAssociationIds and never returns all-tenants for non-platform admins); for by-id read/write, load the request, then assertAssociationScope(req, request.associationId) BEFORE returning/mutating (or add assertResourceScope with a new 'records-request' resolver case — provided A-AUTHZ-001 is fixed to fail closed); for create, force associationId from validated scope and reject client-supplied cross-tenant associationId.
regression_risk: Low-to-medium — adding scope checks may 400/403 existing clients that relied on omitting associationId (which currently returns all tenants), so the frontend must always pass a scoped associationId; platform-admin cross-tenant flows must be preserved.
tests_required: tenant A admin GET /api/records-requests/:id of a tenant B request returns 403/404
tenant A admin PATCH of tenant B request denied
GET /api/records-requests without associationId does not return other tenants' rows for a non-platform admin
POST cannot create a request in a non-scoped association
platform-admin retains cross-tenant access
limitations: Static analysis only; did not execute requests. HTTP-level behavior (exact status codes, whether an upstream global middleware injects scoping) was inferred from the route + storage code, which is unambiguous here — marked validated but a runtime PoC would make it airtight.

title: amenities module: list endpoint trusts raw client associationId and by-id write/read endpoints have no association check — cross-tenant IDOR (amenities + owner reservations)
summary: server/routes/amenities.ts enforces role but not tenant scope. GET /api/amenities reads a raw req.query.associationId (via p()) and queries WHERE amenities.associationId = <that raw client value> with no membership validation (not getAssociationIdQuery, not assertAssociationScope) — so any admin can list any tenant's amenities by supplying that tenant's associationId. The by-id handlers PATCH /api/amenities/:id, DELETE /api/amenities/:id, GET /api/amenities/:id/reservations, PATCH /api/amenity-reservations/:id, GET /api/amenities/:id/blocks all query WHERE amenities.id = <raw id> / WHERE amenityReservations.id = <raw id> with no association gate — classic cross-tenant IDOR for update/delete of amenities and read/modify of amenity reservations (which reference units/owners).
impact: Cross-tenant read of any association's amenities list (supply target associationId), and cross-tenant update/delete of amenities by id, plus read/modify of amenity reservations belonging to another association's units/owners. Data sensitivity is lower than financial/records surfaces but reservations link to owner/unit identity and the write path allows tampering/denial (deleting or editing another tenant's amenities/bookings).
validation: Read the amenities GET handler (lines 76-88) confirming the raw associationId flows straight into the WHERE clause with only a non-empty check. Grepped the module's route handlers (lines 100-220) confirming each by-id handler queries by raw id with no association predicate and the module contains zero scope-helper calls. The module's 18 'scope-refs' counted by the heuristic are all `.where(eq(amenities.associationId, <unvalidated input>))` filters, not membership validations.
remediation_direction: GET list: replace the raw read with getAssociationIdQuery(req) (validates the requested id against scope and defaults to the admin's single scope). By-id handlers: after loading the amenity/reservation, call assertAssociationScope(req, row.associationId) before mutating/returning (or route through assertResourceScope with a new 'amenity'/'amenity-reservation' resolver case, contingent on A-AUTHZ-001 fail-closed fix). Reject client-supplied associationId on create/update.
regression_risk: Low — the frontend already passes associationId on list; adding validation only rejects out-of-scope values. By-id checks add a single membership assertion per handler.
tests_required: tenant A admin GET /api/amenities?associationId=<tenantB> returns 403
tenant A admin PATCH/DELETE /api/amenities/:id of a tenant B amenity denied
tenant A admin cannot read tenant B amenity reservations
platform-admin retains cross-tenant access
limitations: Static analysis only; did not execute requests. Did not fully read amenity reservation write body handling for additional trusted-input issues; focused on the isolation gap. amenities data sensitivity assessed as moderate (public-ish facility data + owner reservation linkage).

title: Systemic inconsistency: newer server/routes/*.ts modules hand-roll Drizzle queries and bypass the central tenant-isolation guards, applying isolation unevenly
summary: The main server/routes.ts consistently routes tenant scope through three central, fail-closed-ish guards (getAssociationIdQuery validates query param against req.adminScopedAssociationIds and throws on out-of-scope; assertAssociationScope; assertResourceScope). The extracted per-feature modules under server/routes/*.ts do NOT uniformly use these — several build raw db.select()/db.update() queries and re-implement (or omit) scoping ad hoc. Of the sampled modules, meeting-prep.ts, stripe-connect.ts, and pressing-items.ts DO validate (assertAssociationScope / scoped.includes / platformAdminOnly), while records-requests.ts (A-AUTHZ-002) and amenities.ts (A-AUTHZ-003) omit it entirely. This architectural drift is the root enabler of the two confirmed module IDORs and means new modules are one forgotten check away from a cross-tenant leak.
evidence:
Central safe guard server/routes.ts:786-811 getAssociationIdQuery — validates `if (!scopedAssociationIds.includes(requested)) throw new Error("Requested association is outside admin scope")`.
Safe module example server/routes/meeting-prep.ts:103-107 — reads raw query then `assertAssociationScope(req, associationId);`.
Safe module example server/routes/pressing-items.ts:154-155 — `if (req.adminRole !== "platform-admin" && !scoped.includes(associationId)) return 403`.
Unsafe modules — records-requests.ts and amenities.ts (see A-AUTHZ-002/003): zero central-guard usage, raw id/param queries.
impact: No isolation enforcement standard is applied at the module boundary, so tenant isolation depends on each module author remembering to add a check. This produced at least two confirmed cross-tenant IDOR modules and creates ongoing regression risk as new server/routes/*.ts modules are added.
validation: Per-module counted route definitions vs scope-reference density, then manually read the lowest-density modules (records-requests, amenities) and spot-checked mid/high-density modules (meeting-prep, pressing-items, stripe-connect, plaid handlers in main routes) to confirm which validate and which do not. Confirmed the central guards in routes.ts are correct/fail-closed; the inconsistency is in adoption, not in the guards themselves.
remediation_direction: Establish a single mandatory isolation primitive that every route module must call (e.g. a resolveScopedAssociationId(req) that always validates against req.adminScopedAssociationIds, plus assertResourceScope hardened to fail-closed per A-AUTHZ-001), and add a lint/CI check or a shared middleware that fails routes lacking an association assertion. Audit all remaining server/routes/*.ts modules (only ~7 fully read here) for the same gap.
regression_risk: Low as a policy/refactor if introduced additively; each converted module needs the same tenant-scope tests.
tests_required: a cross-tenant IDOR test harness applied to every server/routes/*.ts module
CI check that flags a route handler doing db.select/update by id with no association assertion
limitations: Only 7 of 16 sub-route modules were read in full (records-requests, amenities, meeting-prep, pressing-items, stripe-connect, plus main-routes plaid/dashboard/governance samples); the remaining modules (account-statement, agent-actions, admin-disbursements, admin-payments, ai-assistant, admin-reconciliation, payment-portal, resale-certificate, autopay, violation-triage, observability-smoke-test) were only scope-ref-density triaged, not line-audited. The main routes.ts (~515 handlers) was sampled by grep-cluster, not exhaustively read — additional un-guarded by-id handlers may exist and need dedicated review. Owner-portal routes (PortalRequest) were not audited in this lane.

title: Amenity deposit forfeit (capture) and refund (cancel) send NO Idempotency-Key, causing money/record desync on retry
summary: The two amenity deposit-resolution Stripe calls that MOVE money — forfeitDeposit (PaymentIntent capture) and refundDeposit (PaymentIntent cancel) — pass NO Idempotency-Key, unlike chargeFee (`amn-fee-`) and holdDeposit (`amn-deposit-`) which do. This violates the codebase's own stated invariant in stripe-idempotency.ts: 'Every Stripe POST that CREATES or MOVES money must carry a stable Idempotency-Key.' A dedicated `refundKey` helper exists but is never used by the amenity gateway.
impact: Stripe's native capture/cancel semantics prevent a literal double-capture (a 2nd capture on an already-captured intent errors), so this is NOT a double-money bug. The real damage is money-record desync: forfeitDeposit captures the hold (money → HOA income) or refundDeposit releases it, then the DB column write in resolveAmenityDeposit can fail or the process can crash. On the caller's retry, forfeitDeposit/refundDeposit fails at Stripe (already captured/canceled → ok:false), so depositForfeitedCents/depositRefundedCents is NEVER incremented. Result: money forfeited to income but the reservation still shows the deposit as held (phantom liability / understated GL income), or the deposit released but the books still show it held. No reconciler back-fills the columns from Stripe truth.
validation: Confirmed by reading amenity-stripe-gateway.ts (money ops), amenity-money-service.ts resolveAmenityDeposit (column write only on ok:true), and stripe-idempotency.ts (refundKey exported but unused here). Only caller is server/routes/amenities.ts:190, which swallows resolution errors as 'non-fatal'. No webhook/reconciler updates these columns.
remediation_direction: Add a stable Idempotency-Key to both forfeit and refund (e.g. `amn-forfeit-${reservationId}-${amountCents}` / `amn-refund-${reservationId}-${amountCents}`, or route through the existing stripe-idempotency helper), AND persist the deposit-hold PaymentIntent id on the reservation so a retry can re-check Stripe state rather than blindly re-issuing. Consider a reconciler that reads Stripe intent status back into the columns.
regression_risk: Low — additive header on money-moving POSTs; must ensure the key encodes the exact operation grain (amount included) so a legitimate distinct forfeit/refund of a different amount is a distinct key.
tests_required: forfeit retry after DB write failure does not double-capture and the column eventually reflects the captured amount
refund retry after cancel does not error-loop and column reflects release
distinct partial forfeits of different amounts produce distinct keys
limitations: Currently latent: the entire amenity money loop is gated OFF by default (isGlEnabledForAssociation → GL_ENABLED/GL_ENABLED_ASSOCIATIONS both default empty, incl. CHC). Becomes live the moment an association is allowlisted. Exact real-world failure frequency needs runtime verification.

title: Non-transactional charge-then-column-write in the amenity money loop with no Stripe-truth reconciler
summary: In both captureAmenityBookingMoney (fee/hold) and resolveAmenityDeposit (refund/forfeit), the Stripe money op executes FIRST and the DB money-column write happens AFTER, in a separate non-atomic step. A DB failure or process crash between a successful Stripe op and the column write leaves money moved but the reservation columns at 0. Both call sites in routes/amenities.ts wrap the money loop in try/catch and treat errors as 'non-fatal', swallowing the inconsistency. No webhook or reconciler reads Stripe truth back into feeChargedCents/depositHeldCents/depositForfeitedCents/depositRefundedCents.
impact: Money captured at Stripe but reservation columns/GL show 0 → income not recorded, or a held deposit not recorded. For fee/hold, a caller retry within Stripe's 24h idempotency window is safe (amn-fee-/amn-deposit- keys collapse the charge), so the window is: DB write fails AND no retry within 24h. For forfeit/refund the retry itself fails (see A-STRIPE-001), so the column can never self-heal. There is no reconciliation loop that repairs the columns from Stripe.
validation: Read both service functions end-to-end and both call sites; grep confirms these are the only callers and no webhook/reconciler touches the four money columns.
remediation_direction: Either (a) wrap the DB column write + Stripe op so the record is written idempotently keyed on the Stripe intent id and reconciled from Stripe on any mismatch, or (b) add a reconciler that lists reservations whose Stripe intents (by metadata[amenityReservationId]) disagree with the stored columns and repairs them. Persist the intent id at hold time to make repair deterministic.
regression_risk: Medium — introduces a reconciliation path over money columns; must be idempotent and read-only against the ledger of record.
tests_required: simulate DB write failure after Stripe success and assert reconciler repairs the column
assert repair never double-writes when column already correct
limitations: Latent while GL flag is off (default). Whether a crash-between-steps has occurred in practice needs runtime/log verification; this is a design defect, not observed data loss.

title: Deposit-hold lookup relies on eventually-consistent Stripe Search instead of a stored intent id
summary: refundDeposit and forfeitDeposit locate the deposit-hold PaymentIntent via the Stripe Search API (`/payment_intents/search` by metadata), which is eventually consistent (search results can lag creation by a noticeable interval). The intent id is deliberately NOT persisted ('forward-only: no migration'). A refund/forfeit issued shortly after the hold — or any same-session resolution — can return no hit → `deposit-hold-not-found`, so the money op is skipped and the column stays unwritten while the deposit remains authorized.
impact: A deposit that should be refunded/forfeited is left authorized (or the auth eventually expires and silently releases with no forfeit ever recorded). Combined with A-STRIPE-002, the column stays 0 and the record diverges from money reality. Typical amenity flow resolves deposits days later (propagation not an issue), so this bites the rapid-resolution / test-in-prod / same-session paths.
validation: Read findDepositHoldIntent and both callers. Stripe Search API eventual-consistency is documented behavior; the code has no retry/poll on an empty result.
remediation_direction: Persist the deposit-hold PaymentIntent id on the reservation (a nullable column) at hold time and look it up directly by id (strongly consistent). Keep the metadata search only as a fallback with a bounded retry.
regression_risk: Low-medium — adds a column + write; direct-id lookup is more robust than search.
tests_required: hold then immediately forfeit resolves via stored id without relying on search
search fallback still works when id is absent
limitations: Latent while GL flag off. Real-world lag magnitude needs runtime verification against Stripe.

title: Platform subscription-billing Stripe passthrough has no idempotency support and no transient-error retry
summary: The internal `stripeRequest(method, path, body)` helper used for the platform SaaS-billing flow (customer create, subscription create, checkout-session create, meter events) never accepts or sends an Idempotency-Key, and there is no 429/5xx retry/backoff anywhere in the Stripe layer. A network retry of a subscription/customer create can create duplicate Stripe customers or subscriptions. Guarded only by a pre-Stripe DB existence check (getPlatformSubscription → 409), which is a TOCTOU window: the Stripe subscription can be created before the local row is written, so a retry (existing still null) issues a second subscription.
impact: Duplicate Stripe customers (cosmetic/ops noise) and — worst case — a duplicate active subscription double-billing an HOA for the YCM SaaS. The direct-create route is platform-admin-gated (requireAdminRole ['platform-admin']) which lowers exposure; the public signup path creates a Checkout Session the user completes once (lower risk). Transient Stripe 5xx surfaces as a hard error to the caller with no in-process retry.
validation: Read stripeRequest and all 12 call sites; confirmed no idempotency header path exists and the create route relies on a pre-Stripe DB check.
remediation_direction: Extend stripeRequest to accept an optional idempotencyKey and set the header; supply a stable key on customer/subscription/checkout creates keyed by associationId+plan+period. Add bounded retry with backoff on 429/5xx (safe once idempotency keys are present).
regression_risk: Low — additive; keys must be stable per logical operation.
tests_required: subscription create retry does not produce a second Stripe subscription
customer create retry reuses the customer
limitations: The dues/owner-payment paths (payment-service.ts, owner payment link) DO carry idempotency keys and are unaffected; this finding is scoped to the platform-billing passthrough only.

title: refundKey collapses two distinct same-amount partial refunds of one charge within Stripe's 24h idempotency window
summary: The dues refund idempotency key is `ycm:refund:<chargeId>:<amountCents|full>`. This correctly collapses a retry of the SAME refund, but it also collapses two LEGITIMATELY DISTINCT partial refunds of equal magnitude against the same charge (e.g. refund $25, then a separate $25 later) issued within Stripe's ~24h idempotency window — Stripe replays the first refund and silently does NOT issue the second.
impact: A resident/owner owed two separate equal-magnitude refunds on the same charge within 24h receives only one; the second is silently absorbed. The helper's own doc frames equal-amount as a 'retry', conflating a retry with a distinct second refund. Narrow but real money-out correctness edge.
validation: Read refundKey and its single call site in refund-service.ts. This is a fact about the key grain; real-world trigger frequency is low (two equal partial refunds on one charge inside 24h).
remediation_direction: Add a per-refund disambiguator to the key (a refund-request id / attempt nonce) so a distinct refund operation is a distinct key, while still collapsing true network retries of the same request. Only include amount for the retry-collapse semantics of a single logical refund.
regression_risk: Low — key-grain change; must preserve retry-collapse for genuine retries.
tests_required: two distinct $X refunds on the same charge both succeed
a true network retry of one refund still collapses
limitations: Depends on Stripe's 24h idempotency-window replay semantics; confirm exact window/behavior at runtime. Documented as an intended tradeoff in stripe-idempotency.ts, so may be by-design — flag for product decision.

title: Cleared: generic Stripe passthrough is not a client-controlled price-tampering vector; owner-payment amounts are server-capped
summary: Investigated the flagged 'generic Stripe passthrough' (routes.ts:16245) and client-amount surfaces for price tampering; found them adequately guarded. (1) stripeRequest's `path`/`body` are constructed by named internal callers from server-side ids (subscription ids, session ids, hardcoded literals) — no HTTP route forwards a raw client `path`/amount into it. (2) The owner-payment-link checkout accepts a client `amount` but hard-caps it server-side to `min(outstandingBalance, link.amount)` and rejects `amount > maxAllowedAmount` and non-exact amounts when partial pay is disabled. (3) The only client `priceId` override is the platform-subscription create route, gated by requireAdminRole(['platform-admin']); public signup resolves priceId from a server planCatalog lookup.
impact: No client-facing price-tampering or amount-inflation vector found in the audited paths. Amount math is integer-cents throughout the dues/amenity paths (no parseFloat on money; Math.round(amount*100) used correctly), so no float-drift on charge amounts.
validation: Read stripeRequest and all 12 call sites, the owner-payment-link POST handler in full, the dues checkout/off-session builders, and the subscription create route auth guards.
remediation_direction: No action required for tampering. Optional hardening: assert `amountCents` integer bounds server-side before every Stripe POST as defense-in-depth.
regression_risk: None (informational).
limitations: Scope limited to the Stripe money paths in payment-service.ts, amenity-stripe-gateway.ts, refund-service.ts, and routes.ts Stripe call sites. Did not exercise runtime; webhook-side reconciliation (payout.paid explosion, checkout.session.completed) was not deep-audited in this lane.

title: Owner/board portal bearer (x-portal-access-id) is a permanent, non-rotating, non-revocable DB primary key
summary: The owner portal auth token is the portal_access row's primary key (`gen_random_uuid()`), returned to the client by /api/portal/verify-login and replayed on every request in the `x-portal-access-id` header. It is high-entropy (122-bit UUIDv4, so NOT enumerable), but it is a PERMANENT bearer: it never rotates, there is NO server-side portal logout/revocation endpoint, and requirePortal calls touchPortalAccessLogin() on every request which refreshes lastLoginAt — so the only expiry (30-day inactivity in resolvePortalAccessContext) is a rolling window that never fires for an actively-used (or attacker-used) id. The same UUID doubles as a foreign key referenced across many tables (documents, contact-update requests, incident reports, push_subscriptions), widening its leak surface, and it is carried as a custom header rather than an httpOnly/Secure/SameSite cookie.
evidence: requirePortal: `const portalAccessId = req.header("x-portal-access-id") || ""; ... const resolved = await storage.resolvePortalAccessContext(portalAccessId); ... await storage.touchPortalAccessLogin(access.id);` (routes.ts:1296,1298,1315). Schema: `id: varchar("id").primaryKey().default(sql\`gen_random_uuid()\`)` (schema.ts:1538). Refresh: `touchPortalAccessLogin(id){ await db.update(portalAccess).set({ lastLoginAt: new Date(), ... }) }` (storage.ts:13505-13507). Expiry only on inactivity: `if (elapsed > SESSION_MAX_AGE_MS) return undefined;` (storage.ts:13358-13360). No `portal/logout` route exists (grep returned nothing). verify-login returns the raw id to the client: `res.json({ portalAccessId: sessionAccess.id, ... })` (routes.ts:13802).
impact: If a single owner's (or board-member's — the same bearer grants board access via portalHasBoardAccess) access-id leaks via any channel (referer if ever placed in a URL, server/proxy logs, browser storage exfil via XSS, a shared device, an emailed link, a screenshot), the attacker gains full portal access to that owner's association/unit data and financial actions for effectively unlimited duration — there is no logout, no rotation, and the victim's own continued use keeps the window alive. Revocation requires an admin to change the access row status.
validation: Confirmed by reading the middleware, storage resolver, schema, and verify-login handler on origin/main. Not runtime-verified. The client-side storage medium (localStorage vs memory) is a HYPOTHESIS — the client was out of audit scope; the server design facts (permanence, no-rotation, no-logout, header transport) are confirmed.
remediation_direction: Separate the bearer secret from the row PK: issue a rotating, opaque, hashed-at-rest portal session token (own table) with a fixed absolute lifetime + idle timeout, bound to the OTP-authenticated email/access, and add a portal logout endpoint that revokes that token server-side. Do not refresh expiry on every request without an absolute cap. Prefer an httpOnly/Secure/SameSite cookie over a JS-readable header.
regression_risk: High — this is the core portal auth mechanism referenced by dozens of routes; changing it touches every portal endpoint and the client. Requires phased migration + token backfill.
tests_required: portal token rotates on re-login and old token is rejected
portal logout invalidates the token server-side
absolute-lifetime cap fires even under continuous use
leaked/rotated id no longer resolves
limitations: Did not audit the client (token storage/transport on the browser side) or every downstream portal route's data-scoping; verified the representative scoping pattern (account-statement resolves personId/associationId from session, never query) and the one non-requirePortal use (uploads-access) which is correctly scoped.

title: Portal + vendor-portal OTP codes generated with Math.random() (non-CSPRNG, CWE-338)
summary: Both the owner-portal and vendor-portal login OTPs are 6-digit codes generated with `Math.floor(100000 + Math.random() * 900000)`. Math.random() is not cryptographically secure (V8 xorshift128+ internal state is recoverable from observed outputs), so the codes are not guaranteed unpredictable. This is materially mitigated by the per-token 5-attempt cap, 15-minute expiry, single-use consumption, and the auth-verify rate limiter (10/10min), which together make brute force and prediction impractical — but a security one-time code should still use a CSPRNG.
evidence: `const otp = String(Math.floor(100000 + Math.random() * 900000));` at routes.ts:13598 (owner portal) and routes.ts:18004 (vendor portal). Mitigations present: `if (token.attempts >= 5) return res.status(429)...` (routes.ts:13735), `await db.update(...).set({ attempts: token.attempts + 1 })` before compare (13738), `timingSafeEqual(...)` (13741), 15-min expiry (13600), single-use `usedAt` (13733,13791).
impact: Low in practice given the layered attempt/rate controls. The theoretical vector is OTP prediction from prior observed Math.random outputs in the same process, but the 5-attempt token cap bounds any guessing to 5 tries against 10^6 space.
validation: Confirmed by reading both OTP generators and the verify-side controls on origin/main.
remediation_direction: Replace with `crypto.randomInt(100000, 1000000)` (or crypto.randomBytes-derived) for both OTP generators.
regression_risk: Very low — one-line generator swap; output shape (6-digit string) unchanged.
tests_required: OTP still 6 digits
OTP generated via crypto CSPRNG (no Math.random)
limitations: Did not attempt runtime prediction; assessed as design/CWE-338 weakness bounded by existing controls.

title: Admin session cookie scoped to parent domain (.yourcondomanager.org) — readable by every subdomain
summary: In production the `sid` session cookie is deliberately scoped to `.yourcondomanager.org` (leading-dot parent-domain) so login persists across apex + app subdomains. Consequence: the authenticated admin session cookie is sent to, and readable by, EVERY current and future subdomain of yourcondomanager.org. Any subdomain that is XSS-able, mis-configured, or delegated to a third party (marketing pages, status pages, preview hosts, a compromised CNAME) becomes a session-theft vector. The cookie also cannot use the `__Host-` prefix because a domain is set. httpOnly/Secure/SameSite=lax are correctly applied, so this is a scope-breadth concern, not a missing-flag one.
evidence: `domain: sessionCookieDomain` in the session() cookie block (index.ts:154), resolved by `const DEFAULT_PRODUCTION_DOMAIN = ".yourcondomanager.org"; ... return raw === "" ? undefined : raw;` (session-cookie-domain.ts:24-31). Cookie flags: `httpOnly: true, secure: sessionCookieSecure (auto in prod), sameSite: 'lax'` (index.ts:148-150).
impact: Widens the blast radius of any single subdomain compromise/XSS to full admin session theft across the whole domain. Documented tradeoff for cross-host login persistence (Issue #447).
validation: Confirmed from the resolver and session config on origin/main.
remediation_direction: Keep parent-domain scope only if cross-subdomain SSO is truly required; otherwise scope host-only. Inventory all subdomains and ensure none host untrusted/third-party content; consider a dedicated auth subdomain + token exchange instead of a domain-wide cookie.
regression_risk: Medium — reverting to host-only breaks the documented apex↔app login persistence; needs product sign-off.
tests_required: cookie domain scope matches intended subdomain set
no untrusted subdomain receives the sid cookie
limitations: Did not enumerate the live subdomain inventory; the risk is conditional on an untrusted/XSS-able subdomain existing.

title: Admin association scopes silently auto-hydrate (read-write) from portal_access rows by email
summary: On every admin request, applyAdminContext compares the admin's active portal_access associations (by email) against their explicit admin_association_scopes, and for any association the admin has active portal access to but no scope row, it auto-creates a `read-write` scope. Admin write authority thus expands automatically as a side effect of portal_access existence rather than through an explicit admin grant. The admin ROLE itself is correctly re-verified from DB each request (isActive===1), so this is a scope-breadth concern, not a role-trust one.
evidence: `for (const associationId of missingAssociationIds) { await storage.upsertAdminAssociationScope({ adminUserId: adminUser.id, associationId, scope: "read-write" }); }` guarded by `if (missingAssociationIds.length > 0)` where missingAssociationIds derives from active portal_access rows by the admin's email (routes.ts:956-980).
impact: If a portal_access row can be created for an existing admin's email against an association (e.g., via ownership sync / invite / auto-provision paths), that admin silently gains read-write scope to that association's admin surface. Likely intended self-healing, but it couples portal ownership to admin write scope without an explicit grant/audit step.
validation: Read the auto-hydrate branch; did NOT trace whether an attacker/unprivileged actor can cause a portal_access row to be created against an arbitrary admin's email — that path (ownership/invite provisioning) needs verification before rating higher.
remediation_direction: Make admin scope grants explicit and audited; do not auto-widen write scope from portal_access. If self-healing is required, hydrate as read-only or require an admin/platform-admin approval step, and log the grant.
regression_risk: Medium — some admins may rely on auto-hydrated scopes; needs a data audit before tightening.
tests_required: admin scope is not silently widened by a new portal_access row
auto-hydrate (if kept) grants least privilege and is audit-logged
limitations: Did not confirm whether portal_access rows can be attacker-influenced for an admin's email; rated low/moderate pending that trace (data-access lane).

title: Inconsistent portal session-expiry enforcement: uploads path checks status only, not 30-day inactivity
summary: requirePortal → resolvePortalAccessContext enforces a 30-day inactivity expiry (lastLoginAt) in addition to status. The direct upload-authorization path (authorizeUploadAccess, reached for /api/uploads/* with x-portal-access-id) only checks `status !== 'active'` and does not apply the inactivity expiry. A portal access whose row is still status='active' but past the 30-day idle window is rejected by normal portal routes yet would still authorize document/file fetches, giving an inconsistent (weaker) expiry on the file surface.
evidence: resolvePortalAccessContext: `if (access.lastLoginAt) { const elapsed = ...; if (elapsed > SESSION_MAX_AGE_MS) return undefined; }` (storage.ts:13358-13360). uploads-access: `const portalAccess = await deps.getPortalAccessById(portalAccessId); if (!portalAccess || portalAccess.status !== 'active') { return deny } ... portalDocs = getPortalDocuments(portalAccess.id) ...` — no lastLoginAt/idle check (uploads-access.ts:124-135).
impact: Low. File access remains correctly scoped to the access's own documents (no IDOR), so the only gap is a slightly longer-lived file-fetch capability than the rest of the portal for an idle-expired-but-active id. Compounds A-AUTH-001 (the id is already effectively non-expiring).
validation: Confirmed by reading both enforcement points on origin/main.
remediation_direction: Route upload authorization through the same resolvePortalAccessContext (or share one expiry helper) so status + idle expiry are enforced identically across every portal surface.
regression_risk: Low — additive stricter check on an existing deny path.
tests_required: idle-expired active portal id is denied file access just like normal portal routes
limitations: File-scope authorization itself is correct; this is an expiry-consistency gap only.

title: Test-only routes are triple-gated and NOT production-reachable (mapper flag is a false positive)
summary: Reviewed server/test-routes.ts (POST /api/auth/test-login which mints a real session, and GET /api/__test/last-otp). These endpoints are gated three independent times, all requiring NODE_ENV==='test' AND PLAYWRIGHT_TEST_MODE==='1': (1) the index.ts caller only dynamically imports+registers them under that env condition; (2) registerTestRoutes() refuses to register unless isTestModeEnabled(); (3) each handler runs gateRequest() which returns 404 per request unless isTestModeEnabled(). test-login additionally requires the email to already exist as an active admin_user. In production (NODE_ENV=production), the routes are never registered and would 404. The mapper's 'test-routes.ts prod-reachability' flag is a false positive on origin/main.
evidence: `export function isTestModeEnabled(){ return process.env.NODE_ENV === 'test' && process.env.PLAYWRIGHT_TEST_MODE === '1'; }` (test-routes.ts:22-24). Caller: `if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'test' && process.env.PLAYWRIGHT_TEST_MODE === '1') { const { registerTestRoutes } = await import('./test-routes'); registerTestRoutes(app); }` (index.ts:505-508). Registration gate: `if (!isTestModeEnabled()) { log('[test-routes] refusing to register ...'); return false; }` (193-197). Per-request gate: `function gateRequest(...) { if (!isTestModeEnabled()) { res.status(404)...; return; } next(); }` (88-94). Second admin gate on test-login: `if (!adminUser || adminUser.isActive !== 1) { res.status(404)... }` (107-112).
impact: None in production given the gating. Residual note: the safety depends entirely on NODE_ENV and PLAYWRIGHT_TEST_MODE being set correctly in the production environment; a misconfigured prod deploy that sets NODE_ENV=test would expose a session-minting endpoint (bounded to existing active admin emails).
validation: Confirmed by reading the registration path, both gates, and the caller condition on origin/main.
remediation_direction: No code change required. Optionally add a hard startup assertion that PLAYWRIGHT_TEST_MODE is never set when NODE_ENV=production, as defense against env misconfiguration.
regression_risk: None (no change proposed).
limitations: Static confirmation only; assumes production env vars are set correctly (the one residual dependency noted in impact).

title: Owner-ledger payment writes use disjoint idempotency namespaces keyed on webhook-event-id/transaction-id, not payment identity — one real payment surfacing as multiple Stripe events (or across both webhook endpoints) posts multiple negative ledger entries (double/triple credit)
summary: There are three independent code paths that each write a NEGATIVE 'payment' owner_ledger_entries row for a successful payment, each with its OWN idempotency key in a DISJOINT namespace: (1) processPaymentWebhookEvent uses referenceType='payment-webhook', referenceId=<webhook_event row id>, deduped only on the Stripe EVENT id (associationId,provider,providerEventId); (2) the autopay block uses referenceType='autopay_payment_transaction', referenceId=<paymentTransactions.id>; (3) writeLedgerEntryForCharge (Connect charge.succeeded / payout.paid) uses referenceType='stripe_charge', referenceId=<charge id>. Because dedup is keyed on the webhook-event/transaction identity rather than the underlying payment (payment_intent) identity, a single card payment via Checkout that Stripe surfaces as BOTH checkout.session.completed AND payment_intent.succeeded — both of which normalizeStripeWebhookPayload maps to status='succeeded' and both of which carry distinct event ids — each pass the event-id dedup and each insert a separate ledger credit for the same money. The same double-write occurs across the two endpoints (per-HOA /api/webhooks/payments writing 'payment-webhook' AND platform /api/webhooks/stripe-connect writing 'stripe_charge' for the same charge), and potentially for autopay (processPaymentWebhookEvent 'payment-webhook' entry PLUS the autopay 'autopay_payment_transaction' entry).
evidence: processPaymentWebhookEvent dedup (event-id only): `.where(and(eq(paymentWebhookEvents.associationId,...), eq(paymentWebhookEvents.provider,...), eq(paymentWebhookEvents.providerEventId, payload.providerEventId)))` (storage.ts:8334) then inserts ledger entry `entryType:'payment', amount: Number((-Math.abs(amount)).toFixed(2)), referenceType:'payment-webhook', referenceId: receivedEvent.id` (storage.ts:8452-8457). normalizeStripeWebhookPayload maps BOTH `checkout.session.completed && paymentStatus==='paid'` AND `payment_intent.succeeded` to `status='succeeded'` (routes.ts:602-607), each with providerEventId=payload.id (a DISTINCT event id per event). Separately, Connect charge.succeeded → writeLedgerEntryForCharge inserts `entryType:'payment', amount:-(input.amountCents/100), referenceType:'stripe_charge', referenceId: input.chargeId` (stripe-reconciliation.ts:154-167). Autopay block: `await db.insert(ownerLedgerEntries).values({ entryType:'payment', amount:-(updatedTxn.amountCents/100), referenceType:'autopay_payment_transaction', referenceId: updatedTxn.id })` (routes.ts:6434-6444). No path dedups on the shared payment_intent id / gatewayReference.
impact: A single owner payment can reduce the owner's recorded balance by 2x (or 3x) — the owner appears to have paid money they did not, association AR is understated, and the reconciliation report's per-owner totals no longer tie to the bank deposit. This is direct money-ledger corruption on a real-money HOA ledger.
validation: Confirm in the Stripe Dashboard which event types each endpoint (per-HOA whsec endpoint vs platform Connect endpoint) is subscribed to, and whether charge/payment_intent metadata (hoa_id/owner_id/unit_id or associationId/personId/unitId) is propagated to the charge+PI objects (it must be, since writeLedgerEntryForCharge REQUIRES it). If both checkout.session.completed and payment_intent.succeeded reach /api/webhooks/payments with metadata, OR both endpoints resolve metadata for the same charge, the double-credit fires. A test harness can POST two validly-signed events (checkout.session.completed + payment_intent.succeeded) for one payment and assert exactly one ledger entry.
remediation_direction: Adopt a SINGLE canonical payment-identity idempotency key (the payment_intent id / gatewayReference) shared across all three write paths, enforced by a UNIQUE DB constraint on owner_ledger_entries over (associationId, referenceType-normalized, payment-identity). Normalize all payment ledger writes through one function that dedups on the PI id regardless of which event/endpoint triggered it. Prefer posting the ledger entry from exactly one authoritative event (e.g. charge.succeeded OR checkout.session.completed) and treat the others as no-ops.
regression_risk: Medium-high — consolidating three write paths and adding a unique constraint requires backfilling/deduping any existing duplicate rows and careful handling of legacy charges lacking a PI id; a too-narrow key could drop legitimately distinct payments.
tests_required: POST checkout.session.completed + payment_intent.succeeded for one PI → exactly one ledger credit
per-HOA payment-webhook + platform stripe_charge for one charge → exactly one credit
autopay succeeded → exactly one credit
unique-constraint violation path returns idempotent success not 400
limitations: Whether the double-credit actually fires in production depends on the live Stripe endpoint event subscriptions and metadata propagation, which are not visible in code — hence 'needs runtime verification'. The code unambiguously will double-post if it receives two metadata-bearing succeeded events for one payment.

title: Owner-ledger idempotency is check-then-insert with NO unique DB constraint on (referenceType, referenceId) — concurrent duplicate webhook deliveries can race past the existence check and double-insert
summary: The 'stripe_charge' and 'autopay_payment_transaction' ledger writes protect against double-write purely with a SELECT-then-INSERT (check existing by referenceType+referenceId, insert if absent). owner_ledger_entries has NO unique index on (referenceType, referenceId) — the only index is byAssocUnitPosted (schema.ts:897-900). Two concurrent deliveries of the same charge.succeeded (or a charge.succeeded racing the payout.paid belt-and-suspenders call, both of which invoke writeLedgerEntryForCharge for the same chargeId) can both read 'no existing entry' and both insert → duplicate credit. Notably the ACH-failure path DOES do this correctly (unique index platform_webhook_events_provider_event_uq + insert-in-try/catch, ach-failure-service.ts:62-84), and payment_webhook_events HAS a unique index (schema.ts:837) — so the ledger-write paths are the inconsistent, unprotected ones.
evidence: writeLedgerEntryForCharge: `const existing = await db.select({id}).from(ownerLedgerEntries).where(and(eq(referenceType,'stripe_charge'), eq(referenceId, input.chargeId))).limit(1); if (existing[0]) return {...skipped:'already_exists'}; const [created] = await db.insert(ownerLedgerEntries).values({...})` (stripe-reconciliation.ts:139-167) — no transaction, no unique constraint. Autopay: `const [existingLedger] = await db.select().from(ownerLedgerEntries).where(and(eq(referenceType,'autopay_payment_transaction'), eq(referenceId, updatedTxn.id))).limit(1); if (!existingLedger) { await db.insert(...) }` (routes.ts:6422-6445). Schema owner_ledger_entries (schema.ts:866-901) declares only `byAssocUnitPosted: index(...)` — no uniqueIndex on referenceType/referenceId.
impact: Under concurrent/retry delivery a single charge posts two negative ledger entries → owner balance understated by 2x. Same money-corruption class as A-WEBHOOK-001 but triggered by concurrency rather than multi-event fan-out.
validation: Fire two simultaneous charge.succeeded deliveries for the same chargeId (Stripe retries + the payout.paid reconcile call can genuinely overlap) and observe two rows. The absence of the unique index is directly readable in shared/schema.ts.
remediation_direction: Add a partial UNIQUE index on owner_ledger_entries(referenceType, referenceId) WHERE referenceId IS NOT NULL and switch the write to INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (or insert-in-try/catch like claimEvent). This also hardens A-WEBHOOK-001 once a shared key is used.
regression_risk: Medium — a unique index will fail if pre-existing duplicate rows exist; must dedupe historical data first. Some referenceType values (e.g. manual adjustments) may legitimately share referenceId=null and must be excluded via a partial index.
tests_required: concurrent duplicate charge.succeeded → single ledger row
payout.paid belt-and-suspenders after charge.succeeded → no second row
migration guard rejects/dedupes pre-existing duplicates
limitations: Real-world exploitability of the race window depends on delivery concurrency; the missing constraint itself is a certain, code-evident defect.

title: Stripe webhook signature verification omits the timestamp tolerance / replay window — captured signed events can be replayed indefinitely
summary: Both Stripe signature verifiers parse the `t=` timestamp from the Stripe-Signature header but NEVER validate it is recent. Stripe's own constructEvent enforces a default 5-minute tolerance specifically to prevent replay. The per-HOA inline path (routes.ts:6376-6387) and the platform Connect verifier (verifyStripeWebhookSignature, stripe-connect.ts:73-91) both compute HMAC over `${timestamp}.${rawBody}` and constant-time compare, but discard the freshness check. By contrast the Plaid verifier correctly enforces a 5-minute iat window (plaid-webhook-verify.ts:217-226).
evidence: stripe-connect.ts:79-82 `const timestamp = parts.t; const signature = parts.v1; if (!timestamp || !signature) return false; const expected = createHmac('sha256', secret).update(`${timestamp}.${rawBody}`).digest('hex');` — timestamp used only as HMAC input, never range-checked. routes.ts:6376 `const { timestamp, signature } = parseStripeSignature(stripeSignature); if (!timestamp || !signature) return 403;` then HMAC compare at 6380-6387 with no age check. Compare to plaid-webhook-verify.ts:221 `const ageS = Math.floor(Date.now()/1000) - claims.iat; if (ageS > REPLAY_WINDOW_S) throw`.
impact: An attacker who captures a valid signed Stripe webhook body (e.g. via a logging/proxy leak) can replay it. On the per-HOA endpoint the payment_webhook_events unique index makes exact-event replay idempotent (low impact); on the Connect endpoint charge.succeeded replay is only guarded by the check-then-insert ledger idempotency (A-WEBHOOK-002), so a concurrent replay could contribute to a double-insert. Defense-in-depth gap flagged by Stripe's own guidance.
validation: Send a validly-signed event with a `t=` far in the past; current code accepts it. Trivially verifiable in unit test.
remediation_direction: After signature match, reject when `Math.abs(nowSeconds - Number(timestamp)) > toleranceSeconds` (default 300), mirroring Stripe SDK constructEvent and the existing Plaid pattern.
regression_risk: Low — additive check; only rejects stale/clock-skewed deliveries (make tolerance configurable).
tests_required: stale timestamp rejected
fresh timestamp accepted
clock-skew within tolerance accepted
limitations: Idempotency (unique index on payment_webhook_events) already blunts same-event replay on the per-HOA path; primary residual risk is on the Connect path combined with A-WEBHOOK-002.

title: Bank credits are never marked consumed (reconciledToPaymentTransactionId is written nowhere) — across sync runs one bank deposit can settle multiple same-amount ledger entries
summary: The auto-matcher selects candidate bank credits with `isNull(bankTransactions.reconciledToPaymentTransactionId)` (plaid-reconciliation.ts:78), but applyMatch only sets the link on the LEDGER side (`ownerLedgerEntries.bankTransactionId = credit.id, settledAt = now`, lines 170-181) and NEVER sets bankTransactions.reconciledToPaymentTransactionId. A repo-wide grep confirms that column is only ever READ, never written. Consequently every matched bank credit remains 'unmatched' in the matcher's own input query and is re-evaluated on every subsequent sync run. The only thing preventing re-consumption is exhaustion of pending ledger entries (settled entries are excluded). When two pending plaid-pay-intent entries of the same amount fall within the date window, run 1 matches the credit to entry X and run 2 (credit re-fetched) matches the SAME credit to entry Y — one real deposit settles two distinct owner payment intents.
evidence: Query: `.from(bankTransactions).where(and(eq(associationId), isNull(bankTransactions.reconciledToPaymentTransactionId)))` (plaid-reconciliation.ts:72-81). applyMatch: `await db.update(ownerLedgerEntries).set({ bankTransactionId: input.bankTransactionId, settledAt: now }).where(and(eq(id), eq(associationId)))` (plaid-reconciliation.ts:170-181) — no bankTransactions update. `grep -rn reconciledToPaymentTransactionId server/ | grep set/update/insert` → zero write sites (only read filters in plaid-reconciliation.ts and reconciliation/report.ts:129).
impact: Over-settlement / double-consumption in reconciliation: a single bank deposit can be reported as settling multiple owner payment intents, so the reconciliation view overstates settled deposits and mis-attributes money. Blast radius is currently limited to the plaid-pay-intent flow (portal Plaid pay, default-OFF), so live impact is low today, but it becomes real the moment that path is enabled.
validation: Seed two pending plaid-pay-intent ledger entries of equal amount within the date window and one bank credit of that amount; run reconcileBankTransactions twice; observe both entries settled against the single credit. Also confirm via grep that reconciledToPaymentTransactionId has no writer.
remediation_direction: In applyMatch, atomically set bankTransactions.reconciledToPaymentTransactionId (or an equivalent consumed marker) for the credit in the same transaction as the ledger update, so a consumed credit is excluded from future runs; wrap both updates in a DB transaction. Alternatively enforce a one-credit-to-one-entry invariant with a unique constraint on ownerLedgerEntries.bankTransactionId.
regression_risk: Low-medium — the report already derives matched state from the ledger side, so writing the bank-side marker is additive; must ensure the field semantics (payment_transaction vs ledger entry) don't collide with any other reconciliation lane.
tests_required: credit consumed once → excluded from next run
two equal-amount pending entries + one credit → only one settled
applyMatch atomicity under failure
limitations: Only the plaid-pay-intent settlement path uses this matcher; the Stripe payout reconciliation (stripe-reconciliation.ts) is a separate, integer-cents path not affected.

title: Money amounts stored as single-precision float (real/float4) in owner_ledger_entries and payment_webhook_events — precision loss and off-by-a-cent risk on large amounts and float summation
summary: owner_ledger_entries.amount and payment_webhook_events.amount are `real` (Postgres float4, ~7 significant digits / 24-bit mantissa). Money is stored in DOLLARS as float (e.g. 100.10), which float4 cannot represent exactly. Callers recover cents with `Math.round(Math.abs(amount) * 100)` (report.ts:111-112, plaid-reconciliation.ts:116) which is robust for small amounts, but float4's absolute error exceeds $0.005 once an entry approaches ~$40k+, so cent recovery can be off by a cent, and any place that sums amounts in float before rounding accumulates error. The webhook write itself does `Number((amountCents/100).toFixed(2))` (routes.ts) then stores into float4, discarding the exactness the integer cents had upstream.
evidence: schema.ts:825 `amount: real('amount')` (payment_webhook_events); schema.ts:888 `amount: real('amount').notNull()` (owner_ledger_entries). Recovery: report.ts:111 `ledgerPaymentsCents = ledgerRows.reduce((sum, r) => sum + Math.round(Math.abs(r.amount) * 100), 0)`; plaid-reconciliation.ts:116 `const entryAbsCents = Math.round(Math.abs(entry.amount) * 100)`. Contrast: the Stripe payout reconciliation keeps everything in integer *Cents columns (stripe-reconciliation.ts summarizePayoutTransactions).
impact: A real-money ledger stored in float4 risks per-entry and aggregate cent discrepancies, most acutely for large assessments/reserve entries and for any float summation, undermining the 'reconciled == bank deposit exactly' guarantee. Today's per-owner dues amounts are usually small enough that Math.round recovers correct cents, so live impact is bounded, but the storage type is the wrong primitive for money.
validation: Insert a large ledger amount (e.g. 167772.16) and read it back; check Math.round(amount*100) vs intended cents. Statistically sample production amounts > ~$40k. Note this is a design-level concern; exact live impact needs a data audit.
remediation_direction: Store money as integer cents (bigint) or numeric(12,2); if migrating is heavy, at minimum change to double precision (float8) as an interim and always round each term to cents before summing (already done in report.ts). Long-term: integer cents end-to-end, mirroring the Stripe payout tables.
regression_risk: Medium — a column-type migration touches every ledger reader/writer and requires careful data conversion; interim float8 change is lower risk.
tests_required: large-amount round-trip preserves cents
aggregate of many entries ties to sum of integer cents
reconciliation variance stays 0 across the change
limitations: I did not query production data; the off-by-a-cent risk is demonstrable from float4 semantics but its real-world frequency depends on actual amount magnitudes, so severity is capped at medium/moderate confidence.

title: charge.refunded / dispute.created / dispute.closed webhooks are only acknowledged — they never reverse the owner-ledger payment entry, so refunded/charged-back payments leave the owner balance artificially reduced
summary: charge.succeeded writes a negative 'payment' owner-ledger entry (via writeLedgerEntryForCharge), but the counterpart events — charge.refunded, charge.dispute.created, charge.dispute.closed — are handled by returning `{ received:true, action:'acknowledged' }` with an explicit comment that 'full dispute lifecycle accounting is a follow-on'. No reversing (positive) ledger entry is written. Therefore a refund or chargeback returns money to the owner in Stripe while the owner's YCM balance stays reduced as if they had paid — the ledger overstates collected payments and understates what the owner owes.
evidence: `case 'charge.refunded': case 'charge.dispute.created': case 'charge.dispute.closed': { // Subscribed + acknowledged so Stripe stops retrying; full dispute lifecycle accounting is a follow-on... return res.status(200).json({ received:true, type:event.type, action:'acknowledged' }); }` (stripe-connect.ts:439-447). The succeeded side writes the negative entry (stripe-reconciliation.ts:154-167).
impact: After a refund or successful dispute/chargeback, the association's books show the owner as paid when the money was returned/clawed back — misstating AR and the bank reconciliation (the payout/bank will reflect the reversal but the owner ledger will not). Requires manual adjustment to correct.
validation: Trigger a refund on a previously-succeeded charge and observe no offsetting ledger entry; confirm the reconciliation report no longer ties to the (reduced) bank balance.
remediation_direction: On charge.refunded, post a positive reversing owner-ledger entry (entryType 'credit'/'adjustment' or a negative-of-the-payment) keyed idempotently on the refund id; on dispute.closed (lost) post the reversal + fee. Reuse the same payment-identity idempotency key from A-WEBHOOK-001 to avoid double-reversal.
regression_risk: Low-medium — additive write; must be idempotent and must handle partial refunds (refund amount vs original charge amount).
tests_required: full refund posts one reversing entry
partial refund posts partial reversal
duplicate refund webhook is idempotent
dispute lost posts reversal + fee
limitations: This is explicitly documented in-code as a known follow-on rather than a hidden bug; flagged because it is a live ledger-integrity gap on a real-money system.

title: Portal Plaid pay posts a balance-reducing ledger entry with no settlement and no idempotency (referenceId = bank connection id) — gated OFF by default, but a balance-without-money-movement risk if enabled
summary: POST /api/portal/plaid/pay inserts a NEGATIVE 'payment' owner-ledger entry immediately on submit, before any ACH execution/settlement, with referenceType='plaid-pay-intent' and referenceId set to the bank CONNECTION id (not a unique payment id). This gives no idempotency: repeated POSTs each create another balance-reducing entry, and no settlement reconciliation confirms funds ever moved. The route is default-OFF behind isPortalPlaidPayEnabled() (503 when disabled), and the code comment documents the settlement risk — but the write path is unsafe if the flag is ever flipped before settlement-on-confirmation is implemented.
evidence: `if (!isPortalPlaidPayEnabled()) return res.status(503)...` (routes.ts:20472). Then `await db.insert(ownerLedgerEntries).values({ entryType:'payment', amount:-Math.abs(amount), referenceType:'plaid-pay-intent', referenceId: conn.id })` (routes.ts:20510-20520) — referenceId is the connection id (shared across all of that owner's payments), so no per-payment idempotency. plaid-env-guard.ts:41-58 documents 'an owner could reduce their recorded balance without money ever moving'.
impact: If PORTAL_PLAID_PAY_ENABLED is turned on before settlement reconciliation exists, an owner can lower their on-ledger balance without funds moving, and can repeat the call to drive the balance arbitrarily negative (no idempotency key per payment). Currently mitigated by the default-off gate.
validation: With the flag off, confirm 503. If enabled in a test env, POST twice with the same amount and observe two negative ledger entries and no settlement linkage.
remediation_direction: Keep the flag off until the entry is posted only on CONFIRMED settlement (mirroring the Stripe webhook-succeeded pattern per the code comment). When enabling, give each intent a unique referenceId, add a unique constraint, and only reduce balance on confirmed ACH settlement (the A-RECON-004 matcher is the intended settlement path but is itself defective).
regression_risk: Low while gated off; the fix is part of building the settlement path.
tests_required: flag off → 503
no duplicate ledger entry per confirmed payment
balance reduces only on confirmed settlement
limitations: Default-OFF materially lowers current severity; included because it is a latent money-ledger hazard directly tied to the reconciliation path in A-RECON-004.

title: Full JSON response bodies of every /api request logged to stdout in production (PII + auth tokens leak)
summary: The global Express request logger captures and serializes the entire JSON response body of EVERY /api route and writes it to stdout in production, unredacted and untruncated. On a condo-management money app this means owner names/emails, financial/ledger/payment records, Plaid data, and auth/session tokens returned by verify-login endpoints are all persisted in Fly's plaintext application logs.
evidence: server/index.ts: `res.json = function (bodyJson, ...args) { capturedJsonResponse = bodyJson; ... }` then in res.on('finish'): `let logLine = \`${req.method} ${path} ${res.statusCode} in ${duration}ms\`; if (capturedJsonResponse) { logLine += \` :: ${JSON.stringify(capturedJsonResponse)}\`; } log(logLine);`. server/logger.ts: `export function log(message, source='express') { ... console.log(...) }` — NOT gated on NODE_ENV (only the separate `debug()` is prod-suppressed). No redaction/truncation between the stringify and the log() call.
impact: Every API response body — including auth verify responses (session/OTP tokens), /api/financial, /api/portal/pay, /api/plaid, owner rosters — is written verbatim to production logs. Anyone with Fly app/log access (or any log-forwarding sink) can read tenant PII and financial data in cleartext; logged auth tokens are replayable. Privacy/compliance exposure on a money app.
validation: Traced res.json override → capturedJsonResponse → JSON.stringify in log line → log() → unconditional console.log. Confirmed log() has no production suppression (unlike debug()).
remediation_direction: Never serialize response bodies into logs; log only method/path/status/duration. If any body preview is needed, gate behind NODE_ENV!=='production' and hard-redact known-sensitive keys (token, password, email, account, secret) with truncation.
regression_risk: Very low — removing the body-append is behavior-preserving for the app; only reduces log verbosity.
tests_required: assert log line for an /api request contains no response-body content in production mode
assert a verify-login response token never appears in captured log output
limitations: Did not enumerate every endpoint's response shape; severity assumes at least one auth/financial endpoint returns sensitive fields (verify-login endpoints exist and flow through this middleware). Actual log retention/forwarding config not probed.

title: Google Maps API key committed to source (fly.toml build arg + docs)
summary: A live Google Maps API key is committed in fly.toml as a Vite build arg (and duplicated in a doc). Because it's a VITE_-prefixed key it is inlined into the client bundle and thus browser-exposed by design — but committing it to git puts it in history and makes rotation a code+redeploy operation, and if the key is not HTTP-referrer/API restricted it is a quota-theft / billing-abuse vector.
evidence: fly.toml:32 `VITE_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY = "AIzaSyCsb1tCLccLzdaKgCm4263A32S0Z0LvdR8"`. Same value at INSTALL-OBSERVABILITY.md:95. Repo-wide grep found no other committed keys/secrets/tokens (no sk_live/sk_test/whsec/other AIza).
impact: If the key lacks referrer/API restrictions in Google Cloud Console, third parties can extract it from the shipped bundle (or git) and drive Maps API billing against William's account. Rotation now requires a commit + redeploy and cannot fully purge the value from git history.
validation: Confirmed the literal key at both locations; confirmed it is a client (VITE_) key inlined at build time. Could NOT verify Google Console referrer/API restrictions from the repo — the abuse impact is conditional on restriction state.
remediation_direction: Confirm the key is HTTP-referrer-restricted to app.yourcondomanager.org domains + API-restricted to Maps JS in Google Console; rotate the exposed key; move even client build args out of committed config into CI-injected build secrets to keep history clean.
regression_risk: Low — key rotation + build-arg injection is transparent to runtime if restrictions match the serving domain.
tests_required: verify Maps still loads on the production domain after restriction+rotation
confirm the key is rejected from an unauthorized referrer
limitations: Google Cloud Console restriction state not verifiable from the repo; if the key is already tightly referrer-restricted the practical abuse risk is low and this is closer to hygiene.

title: Error tracking (Sentry) wired in code but inert in production — SDKs not installed, DSNs empty
summary: Sentry is wired via dynamic import on both server and client but is a no-op in production: `@sentry/node` and `@sentry/react` are NOT in package.json (dynamic import degrades to no-op), `VITE_SENTRY_DSN=""` in fly.toml, and the server `SENTRY_DSN` Fly secret is documented as unset/missing. A money app is therefore blind to unhandled production errors (e.g. a 500 on a payment flow).
evidence: package.json grep for `@sentry` → NOT in deps. fly.toml:37 `VITE_SENTRY_DSN = ""`. server/observability.ts: `if (!cfg.dsn) { log('[observability] SENTRY_DSN not set; server-side error reporting disabled'); return; }` where dsn = `process.env.SENTRY_DSN ?? null`. INSTALL-OBSERVABILITY.md lists `npm install --save @sentry/node @sentry/react` as a still-pending manual step; working/e2e-money-loop-trace notes `SENTRY_DSN | MISSING`.
impact: No error aggregation/alerting on production exceptions. Combined with A-SEC-001, the only prod signal is verbose stdout logs (which also leak PII). Prod failures on money flows go unobserved until a user reports them.
validation: Confirmed packages absent from manifest, client DSN empty, server init early-returns when DSN unset. Mitigant: `.github/workflows/uptime-monitor.yml` externally pings /api/health, so total blackout of availability is partially covered — but application-level errors are not.
remediation_direction: Install the SDKs, set VITE_SENTRY_DSN (build) + SENTRY_DSN (Fly secret), verify capture via the existing /observability smoke-test route; the code seams already exist.
regression_risk: Low — enabling is additive; init is idempotent and no-ops safely if misconfigured.
tests_required: trigger a server error and confirm a Sentry event
confirm client error boundary reports to Sentry
limitations: Live Fly secret state (SENTRY_DSN) not directly probed — taken from repo config (empty build arg, packages absent) + audit doc; if a secret was set out-of-band the server half could be partially live, but the client half and package absence still make it inert.

title: Auto-deploy on every merge with immediate strategy + auto-run schema migrations + no staging and no automated rollback
summary: Every push to the default branch auto-deploys to production with `flyctl deploy --strategy immediate`, and each deploy auto-applies Drizzle migrations via `release_command` against the prod DB. There is no staging environment, no health-check gate before traffic shifts (immediate strategy replaces rather than canaries), and Drizzle migrations are forward-only with no down-migration, so a bad deploy or destructive migration reaches 100% of prod instantly with only manual, schema-non-reverting rollback.
evidence: fly-deploy.yml:327 `run: flyctl deploy --remote-only --strategy immediate`; header comment 'Auto-deploy to Fly on every push to the default branch.' fly.toml:28-29 `[deploy] release_command = "node scripts/migrate.cjs"`. migrate.cjs: 'Invoked on every Fly deploy via release_command ... Applies any [migrations] that are missing.' No down/rollback migrations present (drizzle forward-only).
impact: A regression merged to main goes live to all users with no canary; a bad migration (destructive ALTER/DROP or a long lock) auto-applies to prod with no staging catch. `fly deploy` rollback reverts the app image but NOT the schema change, so data-shape damage has no automated recovery path. Highest blast radius on a single-machine money app.
validation: Confirmed immediate strategy in the deploy workflow, release_command auto-migration in fly.toml, and forward-only migration model in migrate.cjs. Mitigants present: release_command aborts the deploy if the migration exits non-zero (protects against migration *failures*, not against *successful-but-destructive* migrations), and a db-backup.yml workflow exists (enables manual point-in-time restore).
validation_note: Health check exists in fly.toml ([[http_service.checks]] GET /api/health) but with immediate strategy it governs ongoing liveness, not pre-traffic deploy gating.
remediation_direction: Add a health-gated rolling/bluegreen deploy where machine count allows, or a smoke-test gate before traffic; require a manual approval or migration-review gate for migrations touching existing columns; keep the release_command abort-on-failure but add a pre-deploy backup snapshot + documented restore runbook as the rollback path.
regression_risk: Medium — changing deploy strategy on a single-machine app needs care (rolling needs ≥2 machines); a migration-review gate adds process friction.
tests_required: dry-run a deliberately-failing migration and confirm deploy aborts with app machines unchanged
verify documented restore-from-backup path against a staging DB
limitations: Did not audit individual migration files for destructive statements; the risk is structural (any future bad migration), not a specific known-bad migration. Live Fly release history / rollback drills not probed.

title: In-process interval schedulers run per-machine without cross-machine locking; can double-fire side effects during multi-machine windows
summary: Four background jobs (election auto-close, admin de-provisioning, vendor-compliance sweep, automation sweep) run on plain per-process setInterval timers with no leader election or Postgres advisory lock. The codebase itself acknowledges >1 Fly machine can run concurrently (auto_start + the '2 machines, one auto-stopped' rate-limiter comment), and bank-feed-sync already uses pg_try_advisory_lock for exactly this reason — but the schedulers do not, so during deploy overlap or an auto-started second machine they can double-execute. The de-provisioning sweep sends deactivation emails and writes audit rows, making double-fire user-visible.
evidence: election-scheduler.ts: `setInterval(() => closeExpiredElections(), 60_000)` + 'Run immediately on startup', no lock. de-provisioning.ts: `deprovTimer = setInterval(...)`; sweep calls `await sendDeactivationNotice(candidate)` + `db.insert(auditLogs)`. server/index.ts:411 comment: 'fly.toml provisions 2 machines (one auto-stopped) ... a load-balanced attacker'. Contrast server/services/bank-feed-sync.ts: `SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock($1, hashtext($2)) AS locked` — locking used there but not in the schedulers.
impact: During any window with 2 running machines, each fires its own daily sweeps → potential duplicate deactivation emails to admins, duplicate audit entries, and duplicated automation-sweep work. Vendor-compliance is partially protected (checks hasOpenAgentAction before filing), but de-provisioning's email send is not guarded against a concurrent run.
validation: Confirmed the schedulers use unguarded setInterval and lack advisory locks; confirmed the codebase treats 2 concurrent machines as a real state and already owns the pg_advisory_lock pattern. Could NOT confirm at rest how often 2 machines actually run simultaneously (deploy overlap duration, autostart frequency) nor whether every downstream storage call is fully idempotent — hence needs runtime verification.
remediation_direction: Wrap each scheduler tick in a pg_try_advisory_lock (reuse the bank-feed-sync namespace pattern) so only one machine runs a given sweep per interval; or gate schedulers to a single leader machine.
regression_risk: Low — advisory-lock wrap is additive; on a single machine it always acquires the lock and behavior is unchanged.
tests_required: simulate two concurrent sweep invocations and assert only one performs side effects
assert de-provisioning does not send duplicate deactivation emails under concurrent runs
limitations: Effective severity depends on real concurrent-machine dwell time and per-call idempotency, neither observable statically. closeExpiredElections and vendor-compliance appear largely idempotent; de-provisioning email send is the clearest double-fire risk.

title: ESLint scoped to client only — entire server/ (money handlers) is unlinted
summary: The lint script is `eslint client/src`, so the whole server tree — including the ~20k-line server/routes.ts and all financial/auth handlers — receives no lint pass. No repo-root .eslintrc/eslint.config file broadens the scope.
evidence: package.json:11 `"lint": "eslint client/src"`. No `.eslintrc*` found (glob no-match); eslint devDeps present but scoped to client. eslint-plugin-react-hooks is the only domain plugin — server-oriented rules (no-floating-promises, etc.) not applied to server.
impact: Server-side defects that a linter catches (floating promises/unawaited async in money flows, unused/shadowed vars, unsafe patterns) are not surfaced in CI for the highest-risk code. Quality/reliability gap rather than a direct vulnerability.
validation: Confirmed lint script scope and absence of a broader eslint config; server files are not included in any lint invocation.
remediation_direction: Extend eslint to server/** (flat config with a server override enabling typescript-eslint recommended + no-floating-promises), wire into ci.yml; triage the initial backlog incrementally.
regression_risk: Low at config level; the first server lint run will surface a backlog that should be addressed gradually, not block-all at once.
tests_required: confirm `eslint server` runs in CI and fails on an introduced floating-promise
limitations: Did not run eslint against server/ (read-only, no install) so the actual defect count is unknown; finding is about coverage, not a specific defect.
